title: Quiz: Near-Term Quantum Machine Learning
description: Test your understanding of NISQ-era QML applications and constraints

questions.question: What does NISQ stand for?
questions.explanation: NISQ stands for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum, describing devices with 50-1000+ qubits but without full error correction.

questions.question: What is the primary limitation on circuit depth in NISQ devices?
questions.explanation: Each gate introduces small errors that accumulate. With ~99% gate fidelity, circuits are practically limited to 50-100 gates before noise overwhelms the signal.

questions.question: What is Zero Noise Extrapolation (ZNE)?

options.text: A technique to eliminate all quantum noise
Running at amplified noise levels and extrapolating to zero noise
Using more qubits to reduce noise
Cooling the quantum computer to absolute zero

questions.explanation: ZNE amplifies noise artificially (e.g., by adding extra gates), measures at multiple noise levels, then extrapolates the results to estimate what the noiseless answer would be.

questions.question: Error mitigation can completely eliminate quantum errors like error correction does.
questions.explanation: False. Error mitigation only reduces the effect of errors through classical post-processing. Unlike error correction, it cannot completely eliminate errors and has fundamental limitations.

questions.question: Which QML algorithm is specifically designed for finding molecular ground state energies?
questions.explanation: VQE (Variational Quantum Eigensolver) is designed to find the ground state energy of Hamiltonians, particularly for quantum chemistry applications.

questions.question: When designing circuits for NISQ devices, what is the recommended approach for two-qubit gates?

options.text: Use as many as needed for expressivity
Minimize them since they have lower fidelity
Replace them all with single-qubit gates
Use them only at the end of the circuit

questions.explanation: Two-qubit gates typically have lower fidelity (99%) than single-qubit gates (99.9%), so minimizing them reduces overall noise accumulation.

questions.question: Which are realistic expectations for near-term QML? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Demonstrating quantum algorithms work on real hardware
Consistent quantum advantage over all classical methods
Developing and validating error mitigation techniques
Exploring potential advantages on small problem instances

questions.explanation: Near-term QML can demonstrate algorithms, develop mitigation techniques, and explore small instances. However, consistent, practical advantage over classical methods is not yet realistic.
